title: Mattingly Direct Paternal Lineage: Stephen de Mattingley (1206 Hampshire) → Hunter Spence
methodology_note: Cross-referenced from: Curia Regis Rolls 1206 (TNA / British History Online); Victoria County History of Hampshire (BHO); London Church of England burial registers; WikiTree Mattingly Name Study (profiles Mattingly-28, -29, -30, -60, -210); 1969 'The Descendants of Henry Mattingly' (Internet Archive); 1902 Fayette County, Texas history book (LOC); FamilySearch; FTDNA Big-Y haplogroup R1b-DF27 > Y14084. Direct paternal Mattingly chain confirmed across 14 named generations from John Mattingly (~1560 Berkshire) to Hunter today, with one POSSIBLE deeper anchor (Stephen de Mattingley fl. 1206) representing the deepest verified Mattingley individual. A 400-year gap remains between Stephen de Mattingley's son Peter (1240s) and John Mattingly (~1560).

generations.label: Earliest Mattingley — Pipe Roll 1167

key_facts: Earliest known person to bear the Mattingley territorial designation
Recorded in Pipe Roll 13 Henry II (1167) as lord of Mattingley
Predecessor of Revelendus

generations.notes: Etymological/regional ancestor. The Mattingley village existed pre-1167; Ellis is the first surviving named lord. Direct genealogical link to Hunter is unproven through the 400-year medieval gap.

generations.label: Medieval Hampshire — Lord of Mattingley

key_facts: Succeeded Ellis as lord of Mattingley
Had three sons including Stephen de Mattingley
Recorded in Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii, British Library
sources: Cotton MS Cleopatra C. vii, British Library

generations.notes: Etymological/regional ancestor. Direct genealogical link to Hunter not proven through 400-year gap, but lineage continuity at the village level is well-documented.

generations.label: Curia Regis Rolls 1206

key_facts: DEEPEST VERIFIED 'de Mattingley' individual
Documented in Curia Regis Rolls 1206 + VCH Hampshire (BHO)
Granted 6 virgates, 30 acres, meadows, and a mill at Mattingley to the Prior of Merton
Father: Revelendus. Son: Peter de Mattingley
sources: Curia Regis Rolls 1206 (TNA / British History Online)

generations.notes: Direct genealogical descent through 400-year gap (1240s–1641) is unfilled — workable research target via Heckfield/Mattingley parish registers (begin 1538) and TNA subsidy/recusant rolls.

generations.label: Last Lord of Mattingley

key_facts: Son of Stephen de Mattingley
Sold the family's land holdings in the 1220s–1240s
After this, the de Mattingley line disappears from manorial records
Family likely continued as yeoman farmers in the area

generations.notes: After Peter, a 320-year documentary gap until John Mattingly (~1560). Direct lineage to later Mattinglys is etymological and circumstantial, not proven.

generations.label: Thomas I's Grandfather (London Burial Register)

key_facts: Grandfather of immigrant Thomas Mattingly II
PRIMARY SOURCE: London Church of England burial register, St Mary Colechurch, December 17, 1641
Lived approximately 81 years
sources: London Church of England burial registers (Ancestry/FamilySearch)

generations.notes: First primary-source-verified ancestor in the chain. Matches Shari's family memory of LDS records tracing the family to 'about 1525' — the actual primary-source horizon is approximately 1560.

generations.label: Thomas I's Father — Sussex

key_facts: Father of Thomas Mattingly I
Lived in Omny Parish, Sussex, England
Per WikiTree Mattingly-30 ancestor chain

generations.notes: Sussex/Berkshire region of southern England, near the Mattingley village in Hampshire.

generations.label: Pre-Immigrant Generation (stayed in England)

key_facts: Died in Sussex, England 1664 BEFORE emigrating to America
His widow Elizabeth McWilliams brought the children to Maryland
She remarried attorney Walter Pake who secured the family's land claim
Father of immigrant Thomas Mattingly II + 3 other children including Cezar (1654-1719), Judith (1645-1744), Elizabeth Dorcas (1656-1749)

generations.notes: Critical CORRECTION: family tradition says he came over with Lord Baltimore in 1660 and died in 1662. The documentary record shows he stayed in England and died there 1664; his widow + children emigrated. The 'Mattingly's Hope' patent went to his sons Thomas II + Cezar in 1666.

generations.label: Maryland Immigrant (THE IMMIGRANT)

person.death_place: St. Mary's County, Province of Maryland
person.spouse: Mary Elizabeth Suttle/Curtis (1675); then Elizabeth Cole (1690)
key_facts: THE MARYLAND IMMIGRANT — arrived Maryland pre-1665
Co-recipient with brother Cezar of 'Mattingly's Hope' 300-acre land patent in Charles County, MD on September 4, 1666
Will filed October 9, 1714, probated January 12, 1715
Bequeathed 'Mount Misery' plantation to son Thomas III
8 named children: Elizabeth (1681), Judith (1683), Thomas III (1688), James (1696), Charles (1698), William (1700), Luke (1702), Leonard Ignatius (1704), Ann (1706)

generations.notes: Maryland Catholic, Charles County. The actual immigrant generation.

generations.label: Colonial Maryland — Mount Misery Plantation

key_facts: Inherited 'Mount Misery' plantation from his father Thomas II
Will written April 11, 1774; probated September 10, 1774
Names sons Leonard, Edward, John Baptist, and Clement as heirs
Mentions 'Mount Misery' plantation and 'Mattingly's Lane' with a water mill

generations.notes: His will is the documentary anchor for the generation between Thomas II and the Kentucky migration era.

generations.label: Catholic Maryland — Ignatian Tradition

person.death_place: St. Mary's County or Marion County, Kentucky
key_facts: Named for St. Ignatius of Loyola (Jesuit founder) — strong Maryland Catholic tradition
Likely the 'Ignatius' Shari mentioned at the very start of the recording
From the Maryland Catholic St. Mary's County community

generations.notes: The 'We're from Ignatius' opening of Shari's interview is now resolved as a reference to this ancestor.

generations.label: Leonardtown Patriarch

key_facts: Mattingly progenitor of the Maryland-to-Kentucky migration line
Per the 1969 'Descendants of Henry Mattingly' book, Leonard had descendants in Washington, Marion, Nelson, Daviess, Union & Breckinridge Counties, Kentucky

generations.notes: Bridges colonial Maryland and the 1785 Catholic migration to Kentucky.

generations.label: Kentucky Pioneer

key_facts: Migrated Maryland → Kentucky in the 1785 Catholic Holy Land migration
Settled in Marion County, KY — heart of Catholic Kentucky

generations.notes: Deepest CONFIRMED Mattingly in the direct paternal line before the Kentucky-to-Texas gap.

generations.label: Kentucky → Texas Bridge (UNVERIFIED son of Leonard Jr.)

key_facts: One of Leonard Jr.'s sons whose descendants migrated to Texas
Names of Leonard Jr.'s candidates: Henry Martin Mattingly, William Cissell Mattingly, or Leonard III

generations.notes: First of the 4-generation gap (1800–1870 Kentucky → Texas). Direct identity unconfirmed.

generations.label: Kentucky → Texas Migration

key_facts: Generation that physically migrated from Kentucky to Texas (likely 1840s–1860s)
Possibly identical with the 'centenarian' (1828–1935) per Shari's account

generations.notes: Research target: 1850, 1860, 1870 census for Mattinglys in Texas + Kentucky departure records.

generations.label: The Centenarian (lived to 107)

key_facts: Lived to age 107 — one of approximately 5 American centenarians of his era
Per Shari: 'my grandfather's grandfather'
1935 obituary should exist in Texas newspapers — Briscoe Center microfilm at UT Austin

generations.notes: Highest-priority research target. A 107-year-old's 1935 death should produce extensive newspaper coverage.

generations.label: Texas Mattingly Patriarch (La Grange)

person.spouse: May Teichmueller (daughter of Judge Hans Teichmueller)
key_facts: Assistant cashier of the First National Bank of La Grange (per 1902 Fayette County history)
Married May Teichmueller — daughter of German immigrant Judge Hans Teichmueller (1837 Brunswick → 1901 La Grange)
Father of Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (1898), Dr. Claude Mattingly (~1899–1934 suicide pact), and Aunt Mamie
Confirmed alive in La Grange January 1934 when Claude's body was returned to him

generations.notes: La Grange branch — German-Texan + Maryland Catholic Mattingly union.

generations.label: Shari's Grandfather (UT engineer)

person.birth_place: La Grange or San Antonio, Texas
key_facts: Named for his German maternal grandfather Judge Hans Teichmueller
University of Texas engineering graduate
Married Ruth (Baity) Mattingly — bridging the Mattingly and Baity family lines

generations.notes: The Texas Mattingly + Baity union via marriage.

key_facts: Master's degree (per Shari)
Father of Sharyn (Shari) Mattingly Spence

generations.label: The Family Historian

key_facts: Family historian; recorded the 46-minute interview that produced this archive
Currently lives in Santa Monica, California
Owner of mineral rights to Reeves County Texas oil land (Wolfcamp Shale royalties)
Married first to David (Hunter's grandfather)

generations.notes: Source of the family memory.

generations.label: Hunter's Parent

key_facts: Shari + David's child
Sibling of Charmaine (Hunter's aunt)

generations.notes: Living, redacted in the public version.

generations.label: Hunter Spence

key_facts: Recipient of this family history
Recorded grandmother Shari's 46-minute interview April 25, 2026

generations.notes: Living. Surname Spence (from David Spence).

migration_path.context: Lords of the manor (Revelendus → Stephen → Peter de Mattingley)

migration_path.place: [400-year gap — likely yeoman farmers in southern England]

migration_path.context: John Mattingly (~1560 Berkshire, d. 1641 St Mary Colechurch London)
Charles Mattingly + Thomas I; family stayed in England
Thomas Mattingly II emigrates as Catholic refugee

migration_path.place: Charles County / St. Mary's County, Maryland
migration_path.context: Mattingly's Hope, Mount Misery plantations; Catholic Maryland community
Catholic Holy Land of Kentucky migration
Direct route undocumented in this research
Pearl Baity + William Alexander Baity (NC origins) settle

data_gaps: Stephen de Mattingley (1206) → John Mattingly (1641): 400-year direct-line gap
Leonard Jr. (d. 1843) → Ed Mattingly (alive 1934): 4-generation Kentucky-to-Texas gap, including the centenarian
Centenarian's name: needs Texas newspaper microfilm (Briscoe Center, UT Austin)
Frost grandmother's identity: needs Shari's mother's maiden name

deepest_verifiable_ancestor.name: Stephen de Mattingley
deepest_verifiable_ancestor.source: Curia Regis Rolls 1206 / Victoria County History of Hampshire
deepest_verifiable_ancestor.caveat: Direct genealogical descent across 400-year gap is etymological/regional, not proven by primary records.

deepest_genealogically_proven_ancestor.name: John Mattingly
deepest_genealogically_proven_ancestor.source: London Church of England burial register, St Mary Colechurch

y_dna_haplogroup.y14084_significance: Terminal SNP unique to all documented descendants of Thomas Mattingly I (FTDNA Big-Y)
y_dna_haplogroup.british_arrival: Bell Beaker expansion 2450–1800 BCE replaced ~90% of Neolithic British male lineages
